options.explanation: Logs Insights provides an interactive query language to search and aggregate the CloudWatch log data.
CloudTrail Lake queries CloudTrail API events, not arbitrary invocation logs already in CloudWatch Logs.
Athena would require exporting and cataloging the data first; Logs Insights queries the logs directly.
QuickSight Q answers over prepared BI datasets, not ad hoc CloudWatch log querying.

questions.question: A model on a SageMaker real-time endpoint must be continuously monitored for DATA DRIFT against a baseline, with automated alerts. Which capability is correct?

options.explanation: Model Monitor continuously checks a deployed endpoint for data quality and drift against a baseline and can alert.

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Clarify run once at training time.
options.explanation: A one-time Clarify analysis measures bias/explainability but is not continuous endpoint drift monitoring.
Infrastructure metrics do not detect data drift.
Guardrails filter content; they do not monitor endpoint data drift.

questions.question: A finance team must ATTRIBUTE Bedrock usage and cost to specific applications and teams that SHARE the same models. Which approach is purpose-built for this?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock application inference profiles with cost allocation tags.
options.explanation: Application inference profiles tag and route invocations so usage and cost can be attributed per application or team.

options.text: Use AWS Cost Explorer without any tagging.
options.explanation: Cost Explorer alone cannot split a shared model's spend across applications without attribution tags.
CloudTrail records who called the API, not per-application cost attribution.
Invocation logging stores content, not cost attribution.

questions.question: After launch, a team wants ongoing assurance of a generative feature's quality, catching regressions as prompts and models change. Which practice is MOST effective?

options.text: Log responses and run periodic automated evaluations on sampled production traffic, alerting on regressions.
options.explanation: Response logging plus scheduled automated evaluation on samples gives continuous, data-driven quality monitoring.
Infrastructure metrics do not reveal generation-quality regressions.
Relying on complaints is reactive and lets defects reach users.

options.text: Run one pre-launch test and never re-check.
options.explanation: A one-time test misses regressions introduced by later changes.

questions.question: A mobile app's end users sign in and must then receive scoped, TEMPORARY AWS credentials to call Bedrock, with no AWS keys embedded in the app. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Use an Amazon Cognito identity pool to exchange the sign-in for temporary, scoped AWS credentials.
options.explanation: Identity pools federate authenticated users to temporary AWS credentials tied to an IAM role, with no embedded keys.

options.text: Use an Amazon Cognito user pool by itself.
options.explanation: A user pool authenticates users but does not itself vend AWS credentials; the identity pool provides AWS credentials.

options.text: Embed a shared IAM access key in the app.
options.explanation: Embedding long-lived keys in a distributed app is a serious security risk.

options.text: Create one IAM user per end user.
options.explanation: IAM users issue long-lived keys and do not scale or provide temporary access.

questions.question: INTERNAL employees must authenticate through the corporate SAML identity provider to access Bedrock with temporary credentials (workforce SSO). Which solution is BEST?

options.text: AWS IAM Identity Center with SAML federation to the corporate IdP.
options.explanation: IAM Identity Center is the workforce SSO service that federates corporate identities to temporary AWS access.
Cognito targets application/customer identities; workforce SSO via the corporate IdP is IAM Identity Center.
IAM users rely on long-lived keys and do not integrate with the corporate IdP.
Distributing long-lived keys is insecure and unmanageable.

questions.question: A regulated company must call Bedrock from its VPC WITHOUT traffic traversing the public internet. Which solution is correct?

options.text: Create VPC interface endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) for Amazon Bedrock.
options.explanation: Interface endpoints via PrivateLink keep traffic to Bedrock on the AWS network.

options.text: Route Bedrock calls through a NAT gateway.
options.explanation: A NAT gateway still sends traffic over the public internet.

options.text: Use an internet gateway with TLS.
options.explanation: TLS encrypts traffic but it still traverses the public internet.
VPC peering connects VPCs to each other, not your VPC privately to the Bedrock service.

questions.question: An application in Account A must call Bedrock resources governed by Account B using temporary credentials and no shared long-lived keys. Which mechanism is correct?

options.text: Create an IAM role in Account B and have Account A assume it via AWS STS AssumeRole.
options.explanation: Cross-account role assumption provides scoped, temporary credentials without sharing long-lived keys.

options.text: Share Account B's IAM access keys with Account A.
options.explanation: Sharing long-lived keys is insecure and unmanageable.
Public access removes the required access control.

options.text: Use the same root user across both accounts.
options.explanation: Sharing root credentials is a severe security violation.

questions.question: An agent's Lambda must use a third-party API key that has to be stored securely WITH automatic rotation and fine-grained access. Which service is BEST?

options.explanation: Secrets Manager stores credentials with IAM-controlled access and supports built-in automatic rotation.

options.text: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store SecureString.
options.explanation: Parameter Store can hold encrypted values but does not provide built-in managed rotation for arbitrary third-party secrets the way Secrets Manager does.
Plaintext secrets are insecure and cannot be rotated safely.

options.text: A hard-coded constant in the function.
options.explanation: Hard-coded secrets cannot be rotated and risk exposure.

questions.question: Security policy requires that a specific IAM role be able to invoke ONLY one approved foundation model in Bedrock. How is this BEST enforced?

options.text: Write a least-privilege IAM policy that allows invoke actions only on the approved model's resource ARN.
options.explanation: Scoping the policy to the specific model ARN technically restricts the role to that model.
That condition enforces that a guardrail is applied; it does not restrict which model the role may invoke.

options.text: Allow all models and tell developers to use only the approved one.
options.explanation: A verbal rule is not a technical control.

options.text: Choose the model only in application code.
options.explanation: Application-side selection is not an access control and can be bypassed.

questions.question: A company must encrypt Bedrock-related data at rest using keys it can CONTROL, ROTATE, and AUDIT. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Encrypt with an AWS KMS customer managed key (CMK).
options.explanation: A customer managed key gives control over key policy and rotation, and CloudTrail audits its use.

options.text: Rely on the default AWS owned key.
options.explanation: AWS owned keys encrypt data but give the customer no control, rotation policy, or usage auditing.

options.text: Store the data unencrypted for simplicity.
options.explanation: Unencrypted data fails the requirement.

options.text: Use a client-side passphrase hard-coded in the app.
options.explanation: A hard-coded passphrase is insecure and not an auditable managed key.

questions.question: A compliance team must ensure customer prompts are processed only within an approved Region and are NOT used to train the base models. Which statement and action are correct?

options.text: Use Bedrock in the approved Region; it processes the data in that Region and does not use your prompts/outputs to train the base models.
options.explanation: Bedrock keeps inference data in the Region you call and does not use customer inputs/outputs to train base models.

options.text: File a support ticket to opt out, or your data trains the models by default.
options.explanation: No opt-out is required; Bedrock does not use your data to train base models.

options.text: Accept that data may be processed globally.
options.explanation: Inference data is processed in the Region you invoke, not globally.

options.text: Self-host the model, since only that prevents base-model training on your data.
options.explanation: Using Bedrock already prevents your data from training base models; self-hosting is unnecessary for that guarantee.

questions.question: A team must filter harmful content (hate, insults, violence, sexual, misconduct) in BOTH prompts and responses, with adjustable strictness PER category. Which Guardrails feature is correct?

options.text: Content filters with configurable strength per harmful category.
options.explanation: Content filters detect and block harmful categories in input and output with per-category strength settings.
Denied topics block specific subjects you define, not the built-in harmful-content categories.
Word filters block specific words/phrases, not graded harmful-content categories.
Stop sequences end generation; they do not classify harmful content.

questions.question: A banking assistant must refuse a specific prohibited SUBJECT (individualized investment advice) even when users rephrase it in new ways. Which Guardrails feature is BEST?

options.text: Denied topics, defined with a description and examples.
options.explanation: Denied topics block a subject by semantic description and examples, catching varied phrasings.
A keyword list misses rephrasings and is brittle for a whole subject.
Content filters target harmful categories, not an arbitrary business subject.
The PII filter handles personal data, not topic restriction.

questions.question: An assistant must ensure PII in model RESPONSES is masked or blocked before reaching users, as a managed guardrail control. Which feature is correct?

options.explanation: The PII filter detects and masks or blocks personal information in inputs and outputs.

options.text: Run Amazon Comprehend on each response in custom code.
options.explanation: Comprehend can detect PII but is a separate custom step, not the managed in-guardrail control the scenario asks for.
Denied topics block subjects, not PII entities.
Content filters handle harmful categories, not PII masking.

questions.question: A public chatbot faces users trying to override its instructions and jailbreak it. Which Guardrails capability specifically detects these attempts?

options.explanation: The prompt attack filter flags prompt injection and jailbreak inputs so they can be blocked.
Denied topics block subjects, not injection attempts.
Those categories do not target instruction-override attacks.
Temperature does not detect prompt attacks.

questions.question: A brand must block an EXACT list of competitor names and specific profane terms from outputs. Which Guardrails feature is the BEST fit?

options.text: Word filters (a custom word/phrase list plus managed profanity).
options.explanation: Word filters block specific, exact terms such as competitor names and profanity.
Denied topics target whole semantic subjects, which is heavier than blocking an exact word list.
Content filters address harmful categories, not a specific custom term list.
The PII filter handles personal data, not competitor names.

questions.question: In a RAG assistant, the guardrail itself should block responses NOT supported by the retrieved source or off-topic from the query, using a tunable threshold. Which capability fits?

options.text: The contextual grounding and relevance checks.
options.explanation: These checks score grounding in the source and relevance to the query and can block low-scoring responses at a configured threshold.
Denied topics block subjects, not ungrounded answers within an allowed subject.
Content filters target harmful content, not grounding.
Word filters block specific terms, not ungrounded responses.

questions.question: A self-managed pipeline calls a model hosted OUTSIDE Bedrock but wants to apply the same Bedrock guardrail policy to arbitrary text at chosen points. Which capability enables this?

options.explanation: ApplyGuardrail evaluates input/output text against a guardrail independently of a Bedrock model call, covering external models and arbitrary points.

options.text: Inline guardrails only on Bedrock InvokeModel.
options.explanation: Inline-only guardrails cannot cover text from a non-Bedrock model or arbitrary pipeline steps.

options.text: Defining denied topics in the guardrail.
options.explanation: Denied topics is a policy type, not the independent evaluation API.
Provisioned Throughput is a capacity feature, unrelated to guardrail evaluation.

questions.question: A regulated insurer needs strong assurance that policy answers conform to its written rules, with VERIFIABLE logical checks rather than only probabilistic filtering. Which newer Guardrails capability fits?

options.explanation: Automated Reasoning checks use formal, logic-based verification against an encoded policy to validate responses.
Grounding checks whether a response is supported by provided source text, not formal verification against encoded policy rules.
Content filters handle harmful content, not policy conformance verification.
Temperature does not verify outputs against policy.

questions.question: Governance requires that EVERY InvokeModel and Converse call from many IAM roles apply a specific guardrail, enforced as efficiently as possible. Which approach is BEST?

options.text: Add an IAM policy with the bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier condition key to all roles that access Bedrock.
options.explanation: The condition key centrally and efficiently enforces that the guardrail is applied across all relevant API calls.

options.text: Route all calls through a Lambda proxy that injects the guardrail.
options.explanation: A proxy is a bottleneck and custom code to maintain, less efficient than the IAM condition.

options.text: Store the guardrail ID in Parameter Store and fetch it per call.
options.explanation: Per-call retrieval adds latency and maintenance.

options.text: Manually set the guardrail in each application's config.
options.explanation: Per-app configuration is not centrally enforced and is easy to miss.

questions.question: Compliance records must be stored so they CANNOT be altered or deleted for a mandated period, even by administrators or the root user. Which configuration is correct?

options.text: Amazon S3 Object Lock in compliance mode for the retention period.
options.explanation: Compliance mode enforces WORM immutability that no one, including root, can override until retention expires.

options.text: Amazon S3 Object Lock in governance mode.
options.explanation: Governance mode can be overridden by users with special permissions, so it does not guarantee immutability against administrators.
Versioning preserves versions but does not prevent deletion or enforce retention.

options.text: A bucket policy that denies deletes.
options.explanation: A bucket policy can be changed by administrators, so it is not immutable retention.

questions.question: An auditor must determine which IAM principals called which Bedrock APIs and when. Which service provides this trail?

options.explanation: CloudTrail records API calls with principal, action, and time.
Invocation logging captures prompt/response content, not the account-wide API caller audit trail.
Metrics are numeric aggregates, not caller identity.
Config tracks resource configuration, not API caller activity.

questions.question: For regulatory reconstruction, a company must retain the ACTUAL prompt and response content of every Bedrock call in immutable storage. Which combination is correct?

options.text: Enable Bedrock model invocation logging to S3 and apply S3 Object Lock for the retention period.
options.explanation: Invocation logging captures full content, and Object Lock makes the stored logs immutable for retention.
CloudTrail captures API metadata, not the full prompt/response content.

options.text: Print prompts to the application console.
options.explanation: Console output is not durable, complete, or immutable.
Metrics do not store prompt or response text.

questions.question: A company wants to keep chat transcripts for analytics but is prohibited from RETAINING PII. What is the BEST approach before storage?

options.text: Redact PII with Amazon Comprehend, then store the sanitized transcripts.
options.explanation: Detecting and redacting PII before storage keeps useful analytics data while complying with the no-PII rule.
Storing raw PII violates the requirement.

options.text: Encrypt the bucket and keep all PII.
options.explanation: Encryption protects data at rest but does not satisfy a rule against retaining PII at all.
Temperature does not remove PII from stored text.

questions.question: Stored embeddings and invocation logs contain sensitive data, and the security team must CONTROL and AUDIT the encryption keys. Which approach is BEST?

options.text: Encrypt with AWS KMS customer managed keys and audit usage via key policies and CloudTrail.
options.explanation: Customer managed keys give control and rotation, and CloudTrail records key usage for auditing.

options.text: Leave the data unencrypted for easy access.
options.explanation: Unencrypted sensitive data fails the requirement.

options.text: Use one shared password stored in code.
options.explanation: A hard-coded shared secret is insecure and not auditable.

options.text: Make the storage public so keys are unnecessary.
options.explanation: Public storage exposes the data and is the opposite of the requirement.

questions.question: A user invokes their right to erasure. Their documents were ingested into a knowledge base whose embeddings live in a vector store. What must you do to FULLY remove their data?

options.text: Delete the source documents AND remove/re-sync them from the knowledge base so the corresponding vector embeddings are deleted.
options.explanation: Embeddings persist in the vector store until the knowledge base is updated, so both the source and the ingested vectors must be removed.

options.text: Delete only the source files from Amazon S3.
options.explanation: Deleting the source leaves the already-ingested embeddings in the vector store, so the data is not fully removed.
Logs are unrelated to the embeddings retained in the vector store.

options.text: Rely on S3 Object Lock to expire the data.
options.explanation: Object Lock prevents deletion during retention, which is the opposite of fulfilling an erasure request.
